title: Seed Germination Days Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Estimate seed germination days by species and soil temperature. Free, instant calculator.
intro: The Seed Germination Days Calculator estimates how many days a seed needs to sprout based on plant species and soil temperature. Germination speed is governed by thermal time: seeds accumulate heat units above a base temperature (T_base) until they reach a species-specific threshold. At 20 °C, a tomato seed germinates in 7–10 days; drop to 10 °C and that stretches to 20–30 days. Soil temperature—not air temperature—is the single most critical factor gardeners overlook. Use this tool when starting seeds indoors, planning direct-sow dates, or troubleshooting slow germination in early spring or hot summers.
keyTakeaway: **Thermal Time (°C·days) = (T_soil − T_base) × Days to germinate.** Example: tomato at 20 °C → (20 − 10) × 8 = ~80 °C·days. Knowing this constant lets you predict germination at any temperature.
useCases: Planning indoor seed-starting dates: knowing tomatoes need 7–10 days at 20 °C lets you count back from your last frost date to schedule transplants.
Diagnosing failed germination: pepper seeds sown in cold spring soil (12 °C) may take 30+ days or fail entirely because they need ≥18 °C base soil temperature.
Comparing heated seedling mat vs. unheated tray: a mat holding 24 °C cuts lettuce germination from ~10 days (18 °C) to ~5 days, doubling turnover.
Succession planting vegetables: calculating when each direct-sown batch of carrots or beans will emerge helps space harvests 2–3 weeks apart throughout the season.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Germination timing is modeled using the **thermal time (Growing Degree Days)** concept. Each species has a **base temperature (T_base)**—the minimum soil temperature below which germination will not occur—and a total thermal time requirement expressed in **°C·days**.

```
Thermal Time (°C·days) = Sum of [ (T_soil − T_base) ] per day

Days to Germinate = Thermal Time Requirement ÷ (T_soil − T_base)
```

**Example — Tomato at 20 °C:**
- T_base = 10 °C
- Thermal time requirement ≈ 80 °C·days
- Days = 80 ÷ (20 − 10) = **8 days**

**Example — Tomato at 15 °C:**
- Days = 80 ÷ (15 − 10) = **16 days**

**Example — Pepper at 25 °C:**
- T_base = 13 °C, Thermal time ≈ 72 °C·days
- Days = 72 ÷ (25 − 13) = **6 days**

---

## Reference Table

Optimal soil temperature ranges and typical days to germination at the ideal midpoint temperature, based on USDA and university extension data:

| Species | T_base (°C) | Optimal Soil Temp (°C) | Days at Optimal | Days at 15 °C | Days at 10 °C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tomato (*Solanum lycopersicum*) | 10 | 20–25 | 6–10 | 14–20 | ❌ Very slow |
| Pepper (*Capsicum annuum*) | 13 | 24–30 | 6–8 | 20–30 | ❌ Fails |
| Lettuce (*Lactuca sativa*) | 2 | 15–20 | 4–7 | 7–10 | 12–15 |
| Carrot (*Daucus carota*) | 4 | 18–24 | 10–14 | 14–21 | 21–30 |
| Bean (*Phaseolus vulgaris*) | 10 | 20–25 | 6–10 | 15–20 | ❌ Fails |
| Cucumber (*Cucumis sativus*) | 15 | 25–30 | 5–8 | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails |
| Pumpkin/Squash (*Cucurbita* spp.) | 15 | 25–30 | 5–10 | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails |
| Basil (*Ocimum basilicum*) | 15 | 22–28 | 5–10 | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails |
| Spinach (*Spinacia oleracea*) | 2 | 10–18 | 7–12 | 10–14 | 14–20 |
| Onion (*Allium cepa*) | 2 | 15–22 | 7–12 | 10–14 | 14–21 |
| Watermelon (*Citrullus lanatus*) | 15 | 25–35 | 5–10 | ❌ Fails | ❌ Fails |
| Beet (*Beta vulgaris*) | 4 | 18–24 | 5–10 | 12–18 | 18–25 |

*❌ Fails = germination rate drops below 50% or seed may rot before sprouting.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Starting Tomatoes Indoors (Chicago, Zone 6a)
Last frost: May 10. Transplant outdoors at 6 weeks old. Start seeds 6 weeks + germination time before May 10 = ~March 20. Indoor room temp keeps soil at ~21 °C. Expected germination: **7–9 days**. Seeds sown March 20 should emerge by ~March 28–29. ✅

### Case 2 — Direct-Sown Carrots in Cold Soil (April, 10 °C)
Soil thermometer reads 10 °C. Carrot T_base = 4 °C. Thermal time ≈ 130 °C·days.  
Days = 130 ÷ (10 − 4) = **~22 days**. Gardener expected 2 weeks and assumed failure at day 14—seeds were still viable. Waiting paid off. ✅

### Case 3 — Heat-Mat vs. Windowsill for Peppers
- Windowsill (soil temp 17 °C): Days = 72 ÷ (17 − 13) = **18 days**
- Heat mat (soil temp 27 °C): Days = 72 ÷ (27 − 13) = **~5 days**
Using a heat mat saves 13 days per batch—significant for commercial microgreen or transplant producers.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using air temperature instead of soil temperature.** Soil temperature can be 3–8 °C colder than ambient air in early spring, causing major underestimates of germination time. Always use a soil thermometer.

2. **Sowing warm-season crops too early.** Cucumber and pepper seeds sown when soil is below their T_base (15 °C and 13 °C respectively) don't just germinate slowly—they absorb moisture and rot, causing total stand failure. More seeds ≠ better results in cold soil.

3. **Ignoring the upper temperature limit.** Germination also fails when soil gets too hot. Lettuce, for example, undergoes thermodormancy above 30 °C, meaning summer-sown lettuce in direct sun may refuse to germinate entirely—even though it's the "right season."

4. **Treating germination days as a fixed number.** Seed packets say "7–14 days" without specifying temperature. That range assumes optimal conditions; actual days at your soil temperature may be double or triple the packet estimate.

5. **Watering with cold tap water (10–15 °C).** Repeated cold irrigation can lower the seed zone temperature by 3–5 °C, extending germination significantly for heat-loving species like basil and pepper.

6. **Planting depth affecting soil temperature.** Seeds planted 2–3 cm deep experience cooler, more stable temperatures than those at the surface. For small seeds (basil, lettuce) requiring surface light and warmth, too-deep planting combines cold + darkness, sharply reducing germination rate.

---

faq.q: Why does soil temperature matter more than air temperature for germination?
faq.a: Seeds are buried in soil, so they respond to soil temperature, not the air above them. In early spring, soil can be 5–8 °C colder than air temperature due to residual winter cold. For example, a 20 °C spring day might have only 12 °C soil, which is below the base temperature for peppers (13 °C) and will cause germination failure. Always measure soil temp with a probe thermometer at seed depth (1–2 cm).

faq.q: What is 'base temperature' (T_base) for a seed?
faq.a: T_base is the minimum soil temperature below which a seed accumulates zero thermal time—it won't germinate no matter how many days pass. For example, cucumber has a T_base of ~15 °C: sowing in 13 °C soil will result in rotting seeds, not slow germination. These thresholds are species-specific and are documented in USDA Agricultural Research Service seed germination databases.

faq.q: How accurate is the thermal time model for predicting germination?
faq.a: The thermal time (Growing Degree Days) model is accurate to within ±1–3 days under controlled conditions, and ±3–7 days in field conditions where soil temperature fluctuates daily. It was validated in numerous studies and is the standard method used by the USDA and university cooperative extension services for crop emergence prediction. Accuracy drops for seeds with hard seed coats or dormancy requirements (e.g., stratification-needing perennials).

faq.q: Do all seeds germinate faster as temperature increases indefinitely?
faq.a: No. Each species has an optimal temperature range and an upper lethal threshold. Lettuce germinates best at 15–20 °C but enters thermodormancy above 30 °C, completely halting germination—a major problem for summer gardeners. Tomatoes peak at 25–30 °C, but above 35 °C germination rates decline steeply. The relationship is a bell curve, not a straight line.

faq.q: What is a typical germination time for tomatoes at different temperatures?
faq.a: Based on a thermal time requirement of ~80 °C·days and T_base of 10 °C: at 15 °C → ~16 days; at 20 °C → ~8 days; at 25 °C → ~5–6 days; at 30 °C → ~5 days. Below 10 °C, tomato seeds will not germinate reliably. These figures align with data from the UC Davis Vegetable Research and Information Center and USDA extension publications.

faq.q: Can I speed up germination at home without a heat mat?
faq.a: Yes. Common household tricks include: (1) placing seed trays on top of a refrigerator (heat from compressor often provides 21–24 °C); (2) using a seed dome/humidity dome to trap warmth and reduce evaporative cooling; (3) pre-soaking large seeds (beans, squash) for 12–24 hours in room-temperature water to hydrate the seed coat and reduce time to radicle emergence by 1–3 days. A seedling heat mat is still the most reliable method, typically maintaining 24–28 °C.

faq.q: Why do seed packets give a range (e.g., '7–14 days') instead of a single number?
faq.a: The range accounts for variation in soil temperature, seed lot freshness, moisture levels, and planting depth. A seed packet's stated range typically assumes optimal conditions (20–25 °C, consistent moisture, correct depth). In practice, temperature alone can double or triple the stated number. Seed age also matters: germination rates for most vegetable seeds drop to below 50% after 2–5 years of storage, even under ideal conditions, per USDA seed viability guidelines.

faq.q: What soil moisture level is needed alongside the right temperature?
faq.a: Seeds need the soil to be consistently moist (not waterlogged) throughout germination—typically a field capacity of 50–80% (soil feels like a wrung-out sponge). Dry soil halts imbibition (water uptake into the seed), while waterlogged soil promotes fungal rot (damping-off). Both issues cause the same symptom as wrong temperature: nothing sprouts. Maintaining moisture is as critical as temperature for achieving the days predicted by the thermal time model.
